mūla.devanāgarī: कुतस् त्वा कश्मलम् इदं विषमे समुपस्थितम् | अनार्य-जुष्टम् अस्वर्ग्यम् अकीर्ति-करम् अर्जुन
mūla.iast: kutas tvā kaśmalam idaṃ viṣame samupasthitam | anārya-juṣṭam asvargyam akīrti-karam arjuna
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: समयस्थाने समुपस्थितं प्राप्तं अनार्यैः शास्त्रार्थमविद्वद्भिर्जुष्टं सेवितमस्वर्ग्यं स्वर्गानर्हं प्रत्यवायकारणमिह चाकी
संकटे इदं कश्मलं समुपस्थितमयं मोहः प्राप्तः
सभये स्थाने त्वा त्वां सर्वक्षत्रियप्रवरं कुतो

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: Where has this defilement (kaśmala) come from to seize you here, at this moment of crisis (viṣama)? It is not the behavior of one who knows what is real, it does not lead toward the higher good, and it destroys the only thing a kṣatriya can leave behind. [Note: Śaṅkarācārya's bhāṣya is absent for this verse; this rendering follows the logical structure of 2.10 onward, where he opens by identifying grief as rooted in the confusion between ātman and body — the kaśmala is thus the symptom of avidyā (ignorance of one's true nature) before the diagnosis is named.]

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: Having seen Arjuna seated in paralysis, the Lord immediately challenges him: whence has this grief (śoka) arisen in you, here in the midst of this crisis — grief that is practiced only by those without wisdom (avidvat-sevita), that runs counter to your welfare in the world beyond (paraloka-virodhī), and that is, at its root, nothing more than the product of a heart gone weak (hṛdaya-daurbalya-kṛta)? Abandon this utterly petty (atikṣudra) thing and rise to fight — for Bhagavān's command is itself the call that restores right relation.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: Whence has this impurity (kaśmala) seized you, Arjuna, at this critical juncture (viṣama)? It is not the way of those who stand correctly in relation to Hari; it obstructs the dharma that leads beyond, and it ruins the only standing a warrior earns before others. [Note: Madhvācārya's bhāṣya is absent for this verse; this rendering follows Dvaita structural logic wherein the jīva's collapse into grief is a failure of dependent relationship with the Lord, not a mere emotional event — the Dvaita gloss on kaśmala would identify it as the jīva forgetting its ontological subordination to Viṣṇu.]

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: O Arjuna, you whose essential nature is pure (śuddha-svarūpa) — how has this defilement (kaśmala) even arrived? You stand in the midst of danger (viṣame saṅkaṭe), yet the impurity is not outside you; it is the delusion that has moved into the space where Kṛṣṇa's prasāda (grace-overflow) should be. The slayer of delusion's intoxication (moha-madhuhantā) now speaks precisely to dissolve what should never have been yours.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: Kṛṣṇa asks: for what reason (kuto hetoḥ) has this kaśmala — this spiritual filth, this fog of delusion (moha) — come over you in a moment of crisis? It is a thing the noble (āryāḥ) do not practice; it stands against right action and the good that follows from it (asvargyam adharmyam); and it will strip away the only renown (yaśas) you have built.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: He who is called Bhagavān is so named precisely because he holds, undivided and without obstruction, all six fullnesses — lordship, right action, renown, beauty, detachment, and liberation (aiśvarya, dharma, yaśas, śrī, vairāgya, mokṣa) — and such a one now turns to Arjuna, foremost among all kṣatriyas (sarva-kṣatriya-pravara), and asks: how has this kaśmala, this thing censured by every learned person (śiṣṭa-garhita), arrived in you? It serves neither those who seek liberation (mumukṣu), for the noble do not practice it; nor those who seek the higher worlds, for it opposes the dharma that leads there; nor those who seek glory, for it produces only its opposite — and therefore it is, by triple proof, indefensible.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

post_generation_repairs.fix: verb-lemma-misidentification (broader heuristic: prefix-√root canonical for all verb-tagged tokens)

so_what_questions: I'm a founder mid-crisis — my co-founder just quit, funding is uncertain, the team is watching me. I feel frozen. Is there a difference between legitimate grief and the kind of collapse this verse is diagnosing?
The verse says this behavior is 'not practiced by the noble' — but isn't it human to break down? Why is vulnerability being shamed here?
As a doctor I sometimes freeze before delivering a hard diagnosis to a patient's family. Is that the kaśmala being named, or is it appropriate hesitation?
I'm a parent watching my child fail and I want to step in and rescue them. Is that the kind of misplaced grief and confusion this verse is talking about?

The verse ties the problem to three separate failures — it doesn't serve your spiritual good, it doesn't serve your worldly reputation, and it doesn't serve heaven. Why is the argument structural rather than just emotional?
Why does it matter that Kṛṣṇa asks 'from where?' rather than just saying 'stop grieving'? Is the origin of a mental state the real issue?

I'm a manager who went soft in a difficult performance conversation to protect my own comfort, not the employee's good. Is that the same structure — noble-sounding collapse that actually serves no one?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you freeze in a difficult moment — a hard conversation, a deadline, a confrontation you've been avoiding — the Advaita mechanism says: ask where the freeze is coming from. Not what triggered it, but what is doing the freezing. If the answer is 'I', trace that 'I' — it is a constructed story about yourself that is being threatened, not the actual you. The kaśmala (spiritual filth) is the mistaking of that story for your true nature. Act from what remains when the story is set aside.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Rāmānuja's gloss names the collapse as 'hṛdaya-daurbalya' (weakness of heart) — not an emotion but a structural failure of relationship. In practice: when you are incapacitated by what someone else might think, or what might be lost, ask whether your action is oriented toward the person you are actually serving (a patient, a child, your team) or toward your own comfort. Grief oriented around your image is 'paraloka-virodhī' — it runs against the actual good you are here to produce. Reorient the action as service, and the weakness dissolves into function.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's tradition insists that the jīva (individual self) is constitutively dependent on and distinct from the Lord — collapse happens when you act as though the outcome is yours to control. In a professional crisis, this looks like: taking a loss personally in a way that paralyzes the next decision. The Dvaita correction is not detachment from outcome but correct placement of agency — you execute the role fully, the result belongs to Hari. The kaśmala is the illusion that your grief has any ownership over what happens next.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha addresses Arjuna as 'śuddha-svarūpa' — pure in nature — meaning the defilement is not constitutive, it is a temporary occupation of space that belongs to Kṛṣṇa's grace (prasāda). In everyday terms: you are not broken, you have been interrupted. The Puṣṭi-mārga (path of nourishment) practice is to return your attention to the source of nourishment rather than to the disruption. When you are flooded by panic or grief, the move is not discipline but return — re-orient toward what feeds you, and the kaśmala clears on its own.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's four-fold diagnosis gives a practical checklist: is what I am doing right now something that (a) a person I respect would do, (b) produces a good I actually want in my life, (c) leads toward the better version of this situation, and (d) builds rather than destroys what I want to be known for? If no to all four, the behavior is kaśmala — not a moral failure but a functional one. The correction is not self-flagellation but the same question Kṛṣṇa asks: where did this come from, and does it belong here?
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's triple-fault structure (fails the mokṣa-seeker, fails the svarga-seeker, fails the kīrti-seeker) means your paralysis is indefensible from any goal you might actually hold. This is the synthesizing move: you don't need a philosophical position to see that the collapse doesn't serve. In a workplace context — a manager going soft in a performance review, a teacher passing a student who hasn't earned it — the 'kindness' that is actually avoidance fails every standard simultaneously: it doesn't serve the person's growth, it doesn't serve the team's functioning, and it doesn't build the leader's credibility. Madhusūdana's point is that the excuse collapses under its own weight.

primary_meaning: Where did this fog come from, Arjuna, overtaking you here of all places? It is not the way of the honorable, it shuts the door to heaven, and it will cost you everything you have earned as a warrior.
